19. How To Create URGENCY to Improve Show Rate Close Rate and Condense Sales Cycle Timelines 8-16-25 Okay, welcome into this week's weekly group call. Today we're gonna be going through how to create urgency. Urgency is a necessity within the process of getting people to show up at a higher rate. Simply put, urgency is a set of highly believable reasons that make the person more probable to make whatever it is that you want them to do a priority. Everybody's life has many priorities at a given time. And I don't know if you've been able to be self-aware of this in your own life. It's pretty hard to have a fuckload of priorities at once and actually make any progress on any one of them. So generally, what happens is we have things that matter to us more than everything else. And we prioritize those things that matter to us more than everything else. So when I say that you have to have a highly believable set of reasons to actually move people into a state of prioritizing whatever it is you want them to do, I genuinely mean it. The counterparty that you're sitting there talking to— through your confirmation page, breakout videos, through your value-dense email sequences, through the hammer them strategy, through the SDR best practices, pre-call, pre-webinar, et cetera—has to be non-selfish. Actually, real reasons to the counterparty that's sitting there listening to what you have to talk about. And we're going to talk about how to find those reasons, how to create those reasons, how to enter. Inject them into the backend selling systems that we encourage that you take action on. Of course, there's many of us sitting on this very call and throughout this group, as an example. That have already seen the tremendous upside of back-end selling systems, whether it be taking action on just one of them and seeing upwards of a 20% to 30% lift in show rates, condensing sales cycles, more pips, higher close rates. Sprinkle some urgency on top of that. And it's like throwing a couple gas cans straight onto a fucking bonfire. It's sick, the level of result that it can help produce. So without further ado, let's dive into it. Can't stress this enough. When we talk about all of this. I'm just going to start by giving you an actual example so you can like see it for yourself. We have. uh, client. This is Jamil. Jamil has... been an example for us in our confirmation page best practices because he filmed 19 individual breakout videos. Just for a quick background on all those unfamiliar with... Jamil and his offers. Jamil's run an offer in the wholesaling space. For a very long time. It's called astro flipping. He did a little over 20 million a year for a good couple years in a row and then that offer recently started dying and he wanted to start this new offer that he calls co-living. For all those unfamiliar, co-living is a very simple offer to understand. It's essentially taking a single family home and renting out the rooms rather than renting out the home. This enables you to make more dollars per property comparatively to renting it out on short-term rentals like Airbnb, VRBO, long-term rentals like just a traditional lease. You typically net anywhere from like $4,000 to $5,000. His worst properties net about $3,000 a month on this strategy. So it's really good. Moral of the story is... you haven't been able to do it in a lot of major cities because of housing restrictions and limitations. Here's the moral of the story. A lot of people don't know what co-living is. And as a result of that, and this is really important to understand. We've- educated them through these breakout videos. Each one of these breakout videos articulates a key question that was coming up in the sales process. Or just something that we wanted to make sure that people knew ahead of time prior to a call or, in this specific case, prior to a webinar. So long story short, out of these 19 total videos, we did see a solid lift in our show rate for our call funnel. And we saw a solid lift in our show rate for our webinars. But to be rather direct, it didn't really create like a 20 to 30% impact. On the call funnel, it was about an 11% lift in show rate, which was below average. The average is a 17% lift in show rate. On the webinar, we only saw a 2% lift in show rate. The webinar sat at about a 10% show rate. After implementing these 19 videos on it, it lifted up to 12%. Rather negligible. And disappointing. I sat there and I dwelled on what was missing for weeks. And I tested many different things, adding text messages. Adding links to the Zoom room, into those text messages, having the setters call prior to the webinar. Nothing was creating the urgency. Or nothing was creating that lift that we needed in order to get people to show up. I tell you guys this all the time when we look at breakout videos or we look at any other backend selling system. Information is intended to create that prioritization in the mind of a user. The right information moves somebody into action. The wrong information or lack of information puts people in a state of inaction. So here's what I decided to do. I used Manus. We've been talking about this tool for a long time. By the way, super early. I'm a good like 10 to 12 weeks away from actually accomplishing this. I just paid a little over $50,000 to replicate Manus, but to also specifically replicate its agent mode. In addition to that, I have interjected all Jeremy material into it. So pretty soon we'll have like our own version of Manus that I'll be able to offer to you guys as in our circle. Members at no cost, so that'll be pretty sick. But anyway, right now, I used Manus for this. Here was what I decided to do. This was the prompt that I gave it. If you want to sit here and read it, it's on the left. That's where you want to keep your attention for now. The moral of the story is: I gave it details of Jamil. I told it about everything that we're doing in terms of the weekly webinar spend. I told her about the show rate problem that we have. I said that I want to increase the perceived priority of doing this now, and I articulated what we've already done, including the 19 confirmation videos, all the other back-end selling systems that we have in place, and the fact that that still maintained essentially that 10% show rate. I then asked it, like: I want you to go out and I want you to find like real-world examples. That are highly believable to make this a priority now. And when you use Manus in agent mode, for all those who don't understand, like agent mode for any LLM is essentially going out there and like literally doing things on your behalf. So you can see here, like. Here's what it started to do. And obviously we can click through each one of these, by the way, and like find its sources for like how it started to conclude to all this. But it starts doing like rather extensive research. The reason we use Manus, by the way, instead of all these other fuck-ass LLMs, Manus has good incentives. It is credit based. So simply put. When we use this, its incentive is to do the deepest level of work because that's when it makes the most money, versus every other LLM that's just a flat monthly rate that's not credit-based. Its bias is its own efficiency. So it doesn't do nearly the amount of work. Daniel Fazio, who's here in the inner circle, he specifically put us on Game to Manus. Shout out to Daniel. Long story short. He initially said, and this was the simple hook of this whole tool, it'll actually read every word of the PDFs or whatever you upload into it. That was more than enough for me to give it a shot. So anyway, I figured it would do well with this specific task. This was the first time I used it for this particular task. What it then produced was what you see on the right-hand side here, which was this script for the confirmation page that I then handed off to Jamil. Here's the simplest way to articulate what's in the script without you having... to read every word. It goes off and it finds that Austin, Texas had recently implemented what they call a two-phase plan to essentially remove. remove density restrictions that have held back co-living developments. And it talked about the fact that, apparently, this phase, this second phase of this particular plan. It literally just happened. It then highlighted that Seattle, Denver, Dallas, Chicago, Washington, D. C., Los Angeles, Miami, and eight other major cities have all recently implemented similar changes— all within the last 18 months. Now, before I continue, let me just be clear. As an example of a highly believable reason, if you're in the market for considering a real estate investment opportunity and you want something that you haven't heard of before, that to be fair. you may have some awareness of, but again, you're not seeing it like all over your newsfeed via ads from gurus telling you to do it. You don't really know a lot of your investor buddies. Nobody's talking about it at conferences that's sitting here executing co-living. When you hear something like this, this becomes an opportunity to take advantage of what we call the first-mover advantage. Okay, first-movers at advantage is a highly believable reason to do something sooner than later. Because the people who typically do things first usually see the most financial upside as a result of doing those things and taking advantage of those opportunities. When you're a real estate investor and you see something like restrictions being lifted that now allow a new type of real estate to exist without even having to say it out loud, but we will say it out loud. What does that imply? Think about this. What does that mean for institutional money? What does that mean for all the REITs? What does that mean for all the private equity businesses that are in the real estate game? What does that mean for the individual investors who see this? That means that money is going to absolutely fucking flood into this opportunity to take advantage of it. How many opportunities exist? Is solely dictated by the quantity of people that are taking advantage of those opportunities. So the more awareness that happens in this specific space, the less opportunities are technically going to become available. As an example of a highly believable reason, that would inspire urgency and make this a priority for you to, in this case, show up to the webinar and have a higher probability to buy. Tell me, as an example, right now to you sitting here listening to this, is that not a genuinely highly believable reason that would inspire action and make you at least at a higher consideration rate? Take action on this. OK, so let me just let me just continue on before we before I. you know, just delve into this further. There is about three to four other highly believable reasons that are worked into this specific script. OK, all of which are the same. In this case, the second specific thing that we talk about is the fact that this has been globally happening for a long time. We talk about the fact that this is pretty much already normalized through European markets. We talk about India, unfortunately. And also, in addition to that, we just highlight the fact that this has already been something that's happened across pretty much everywhere else but the United States. OK, again, we try to emphasize: like this isn't a risk. This is something that you just haven't had the opportunity to do due to housing restrictions. You understand? So then here's all I did from there. I handed it off to Jamil and I told him to film the video. It came out to eight minutes in length. I'll make sure I share this with all of you inside of the Telegram chat. And anybody who's watching this recording, you can find this link down in the description of the video. For those of you that are here watching it live, I also just put it into the Zoom chat for us. What I'm also going to do is I'm going to add the actual script that Manus wrote. I'm also happy to give you the prompt. I want to be very fair in saying the cool part about Manistow is like— you don't really have to be a prompt master or anything. I just openly articulated it. What I wanted it to do and like where I was at— already articulated to you. But anyway, this video came out to eight minutes. Here's the impact that it had. It took the show rate from 12% to 24%. I didn't do anything else. I only added the video on the confirmation page as an initial test. I didn't add this to the hammer them sequence. I didn't add this messaging to the value-dense email sequences. I didn't add this to the pre-call text. I literally only added the video right here. As soon as you opt in, this is what you see. I also want to be very clear when I say this. I went into Wistia and I checked the play rate on this video. The play rate was only in the 30s. So from there, I knew. If I had a 24% play rate, I'm sorry, excuse me. I had a 24% show rate now as a result of only having 30% of people play the video in the first place. I obviously needed to take this messaging and spread it everywhere else. That's when I updated the value-dense email sequences. We also added it to the text. We also, on this account in particular, we have setters communicating with leads that opt into the webinar prior to the webinar itself. And they also became very clear on these specific talking points to ensure that they're speaking to people about this. Here's what we found. Instead of trying to educate people on all of these different, just information-based videos, people didn't really give a fuck about these. They didn't make an impact on the show rate specifically. Until they had a reason to give a fuck to do it now. This is the number one thing that most people really lack throughout their back-end selling systems. When I see you guys take action on these things that I encourage you to do, this specific variable is what lacks the most. And when most of you attempt to do it, you do it in what I tell you are extremely selfish ways. Or you do it in ways that are just bullshit reasons that nobody actually gives a fuck about that would never actually inspire urgency. Okay, like I'll give you an example— one of you guys I was talking to the other day, I think it was Cameron. He licenses his agency model for local lead gen. So he essentially has like a licensing info product model slash like coaching business. And. I was asking him, like, what are the reasons that people would actually give a fuck to do this now? And his reasoning was essentially something along the lines of, like, oh, well, people will care about doing this because they're licensing something that's like already proven. And I'm like, no, dude, people don't give a fuck about that. Like, that's not actually going to inspire somebody to do it now and make it a priority. You know, you have to find highly legitimate, highly believable to the users sitting there watching this shit, reading this prior to the call. To make it a make it something that they actually believe in and that they want to do. So let me be clear when I say this. At this point, let's go through. The process here of looking at what you can do to actually start to develop these reasons. That way you guys have clarity into the action side of things. I've talked to you about Manus and I think Manus is very helpful as a research tool in this case. But not all of you will actually have the function. Of having some kind of research out there that like gives you a reason to do stuff like, as an example, I look at Devin sitting on this call and I think to myself, 'I'm like: Could Devin go into Manus and find some kind of like research-oriented real-world reason that people are going to give a fuck to do podcasts now?' Maybe, you know, like, maybe there's some supporting data out there, like some kind of study that was done or, you know, something that MANIS could find out there through its research function and its agentic mode. To bring into Devin's confirmation page process. But most of the time, you just have to remember, like, Brennan. I don't want to use like the personal situations that you brought up to me yesterday, but I'll like generalize it for people as an example. You know, Brennan. I talked to, I think like, what, eight months ago or like, I don't know, at least a long time ago, simply put, and it made a lot of sense for him to join into this. However, he chose not to join into it and he chose to delay action instead. And then, just most recently, when he obviously pulled the trigger to join in. He openly articulated what was holding him back. You know, he had some issues going on with his family as it related to some medical stuff. And there was something else that he also brought up. I don't remember what it was, but again, it was a highly believable reason that made total sense to prioritize that comparatively to other stuff. The initial reason that he told me back in the day was something along the lines of, 'I've just hired a lot of people. I don't want to have too many cooks in the kitchen.' To give me advice at the same time, you know, which I thought was a bullshit reason. And then, obviously, later on, he revealed like the actual reason, which makes sense. You know, I'm a guy who doesn't really know, at that point in the initial eight months ago conversation, like, why would you just tell me all your, you know, personal family medical shit going on? You know, I fully understand that. But again, my point is, is like. Could I have said anything to Brennan in that example? That would have created a higher priority to action. Well, I'll tell you what did happen over that entire duration of time. His revenue didn't move a fucking dollar. You know, you stayed exactly where you were that entire duration of time. You barely made any more money. Meanwhile, you see people in my group consistently making a fuckload more money. I bet that word of mouth spread to you. I bet that you probably know a handful of people that are in the group that talk highly about it. I bet you also, again, just see the information that I put out there and how it can obviously make sense and lead to additional revenue. And every month you look at your numbers comparatively and you just think to yourself, 'Well, fuck, I'm stuck.' Sometimes time just has to pass, but we don't want that. Like when we look at our paid advertising process, we want to be able to say things out loud that can get the person to convert at a sooner rate. I look at that eight-month duration of time of failing to help Brennan's business out where he could have been making more money over that eight-month window. He technically could have been helping his family more if he had more money anyway throughout that process and solve those medical problems sooner, potentially, if he would have doubled his cash in the meantime. It's my fault. That I failed to inspire urgency in him to get him to convert sooner. Okay. Again, I can't stress this enough. When you see people taking long durations of time to buy from you, or you see people just saying... This doesn't really make sense for me to pull the trigger on right now. Or you see like 50% show rates on call funnels, or you see like sub 20, 30% show rates on webinars. Those are all symptoms that people do not give a fuck to do it now. And that's a symptom that you need to pay close attention to. And the solution is urgency. Okay. There's a lot of stuff you could take action on as it relates to back-end selling systems to improve your processes. And you should. But remember, people still need like a, like urgency is the foundation to give a fuck about all the other information that you're giving to people. Does that make sense to everybody? Put a one in the chat if you're picking up what I'm putting down here. You understand? This is mission critical to be able to achieve successfully. OK, so again, when you look at. Creating urgency. Let's practice here in the chat. Let's see if you can come up with at least one. That's all I want you to think with for right now. Come up with one. Highly believable reason that people should do it now instead of later. Okay, I'll give you an example of one that I would think with personally, so for me, Price increases. Have definitely been a reason that people pull the trigger sooner than later. But at the same time, that didn't work on Brennan. Brennan's sitting here right now paying. A lot more than what he would have paid if he would have joined eight months ago. But think about this. He really won't give a fuck about that. Why? Because Brennan's at a good amount of revenue and with what he'll learn inside of this group. He'll make back what he pays the entire year at a higher rate within one of these random months. It could be the first month. It could be the next two, three months. He'll make back what he pays the entire year within a month of doing some random shit that we teach him and talk to about. You see, so it's like technically that works on some people, but it's kind of like the logic of a majority hook. Right. When we when we create ads, as we've talked about many times in this group over the years, I typically tell you guys that there's like some dominant reason that people will be to your creatives at a higher rate than the other minority hook reasons. And with the most recent Andromeda update from Facebook, I want to be fair in saying like we have spoken about that minority hooks from an advertising perspective are also working a lot now. But here's the thing with the urgency, okay? There's no fucking algorithm update on human beings for what. Creates urgency at a higher rate than some of the minority reasons. Essentially, what I learned the hard way with the pricing increases is that technically pricing increases only worked as a minority hook. Right? It only inspired action. In a smaller amount of people. Than the entire other half of the pie. I have to find an urgency-based, highly believable reason. Okay. That hits a majority of people. Now, here's the cool part about urgency, unlike an ad. I can hit every single fucking angle. Okay, I can hit every single angle. Let me give you an example of another one. As it relates to the inner circle, since you're all a part of this, we call it red flags. And we mean this. A great example of this is Brennan. As I mentioned, I was going to call Leo, but Brennan bought before I had the conversation with Leo. As soon as I saw Brennan's application, I was going to call Leo right away. And I was going to say, 'Hey, man, just want to give you a heads up.' Like, I've already talked to this guy, like, I don't know, fucking eight months ago. He did fuck all when it came to action. I'm honestly pretty scared of selling to the guy because if this is his behavior, I mean. Dude, the inner circle is entirely based on consumption and action. Like we don't do it for you. You got to do the shit that we tell you to do in order to get a result. And every single one of you know. Especially if you've been in here for even more than a month. We kick people out. OK, we care about your money. We appreciate your money, but we don't give enough of a fuck about it to keep you around. If you're going to make me look bad by being a part of this and not getting a big, juicy fucking win consistently, I want people to be. I want this to be a status when you say you're a part of this. I want people to be like, 'Oh shit, dude, you're a part of that.' Like you must be getting paid. You know, I don't want turds and donkeys in my group. So I give a deep fuck about having the right people around here. We are very, very transparent about that. On our back end selling systems. We talk about it on our confirmation page. We talk about it in the sales process. I'll send selfie videos. Have you guys have seen many times over? That I've sent the group as examples. Where we'll just straight up tell people. 'Hey man, the behavior you're exhibiting right now. Is making us really scared to sell to you.' We don't agree with the way you're currently behaving and we wanted to bring it up. Why are you behaving like this? We don't want to sell to people like that. Here's what we believe in, and here's what we want to see from our ideal buyers. Comparatively, and again, that's had a lot of effectiveness. It's worked, and it's something that we genuinely believe to be clear. When other people hear it, as I mentioned, it's a highly believable reason. It has effectiveness. But by no means is it a majority hook. By no means is it a majority urgency-based, highly believable reason. You understand? It's just another minority hook. It's something that will pepper in and that will definitely tap into. But again, it's not that dominating reason. Okay. So again, I want you all to practice real quick. Throw at least one reason into the chat. All right, now Timothy's reason that he believes would create urgency is mass economic forces like the $100 trillion great wealth transfer are taking place. Those who build brands and use AI will capture the wealth. If you don't move now, your competition will beat you. All right, put a one if you would have responsiveness to that. Put a two if you wouldn't. While people are answering, I'll give you some feedback on that one. Typically, the bigger the number that you articulate, the less believable and the less reality-based it is for an individual. I don't even know if there's $100 trillion in circulation right now or if there's like $100 trillion in actual wealth that's stored up in assets. I don't even know. Think deeply about money all the time. I can't imagine what a random insurance salesperson would respond with when they hear something that says upwards of $100 trillion is being transferred. But yeah, basically, baby boomers passing their wealth and retiring. Yeah. And so, as an example of that, like, maybe you would pepper that into it just to make it a little more reality-based. But moral of the story is: Just try to make that more contextual and like reality-based for the individuals that are sitting there reading that. Of your video, if you're like, have the article opened up, and you're going through it from CNBC, and you're breaking it down a little further, and you just explain that that specific reason a little bit more, that could also create more urgency in that example. You get what I mean? I can't just say, for Jameel as an example, that I initially used that Austin, Texas, and eight other major cities are repealing housing restrictions. I need to give like a lot of context to that so people can actually understand it. You get what I'm saying? Okay. Yeah. A hundred percent. I haven't really thought about like the bullshit meter of like, I didn't even know a hundred trillion dollars is in circulation, but that makes a ton of sense is like. Something that would go off in someone's head when they see the ad or that angle you got to remember, like especially if you're selling to regular people, like I can't even say in that example that selling to rich people that they would understand that they'd likely be turned off by that too. But when you're selling to regular people, like the bigger the number, the less reality-based it is for them. Does that make sense? And then, as an example, Eric, from there, I would start to openly articulate first-mover advantage. And I would like to tell about something specifically—something that I guess an example. Could use the classic age-old 'been milked the living shit out of this one which is the gold rush. So here in the United States, a lot of people lived on the East Coast because they came from Europe, and that was where it was most densely populated initially. The initiative to move west was unlocked as we continued to explore that area, buy those territories, and make it a part of the United States. Back in the day, you could actually go into homestead property. That's why in Montana, there's a lot of families that own tens of thousands or even hundreds of thousands of acres. They got it for free. All they had to do was just go and, essentially, say, 'This land is now mine.' It is productive, but what a lot of people also went west for was mining for gold. So, whether it was to homestead land and create generational wealth for the the rest of your entire genetic lineage's future, or whether it was to be one of the first people to buy some land and mine it, to extract gold, precious minerals and resources that the country needed and that we'd also perpetually need. The people who obviously made the most of the people who went there first took the risk, but the risk was extreme. They had to travel across the Rocky Mountains. They had to travel across Indian-owned land that would threaten their life. Those people would kill you if they found you. That was a big threat. There were all kinds of hazardous things. You were driving on fucking horse and buggy for a lot of that. You know, your wood wheel might have broken. It's like the risk was extreme, but the reward was extreme, right? It's like, right now, there's pioneers that are currently selling AI services to businesses. While nobody else has even figured out that that game exists, those people are going to run laps around the people who eventually try to enter into the game. It's like, for example, with me— I openly teach and talk about marketing agencies all the time because I have a moat. I've worked with some of the best people in every niche that I've ever worked. And I continue to do so. You can't compete with me because I continue to work inside of those niches and get even more experience. I spend more money than what new people. Who even remotely come close to trying to compete with me would spend, in years, in a month or sometimes in a day. You know, there's no competition for me, but there's competition for everybody just getting started. You understand? That's first mover's advantage. I have a natural moat as it relates to the time in which I took advantage of the game. When I first started, people didn't know what a fucking pixel was. I could tell people that they could retarget people and they'd be impressed by that and hire me for it. You understand? It's like obviously the game has become a lot more sophisticated as time has passed as well and it's become harder. With this AI agency business model opportunity, that right there is a highly believable set of reasons to take action on it now, and I can support it with data from Google Trends. I can support it through storytelling. That's an example of what I mean by highly believable urgency-based reasons. And then, what I have to do from there, and this is really important. You have to understand this. I have to be able to make it easy for somebody to start participating in the game. You know, I have to make it also highly believable that now's a great time for them to come into the game and take action. And that's an example where I leave off with that last story. I would then close out that story by saying, and you know what? If I got started today and I looked at every single digital marketing agency that existed, I'd say, 'Get fucked.' I'm going to sell AI services to businesses because fucking nobody's doing that right now. And I'm going to get mine in that space. And now's the time for you to be able to do that. You know? Now is the fucking time to act on co-living and to get into this before all the institutional money floods. And that is by far our dominant hook in that example. Nobody gives a fuck that somebody's doing it over in Europe. Europe's like Disneyland for America in terms of how we look at it. We're selling to Americans. You understand— no offense to all the Europeans in the group— my point being, though, that listening to what's happening in Europe doesn't really mean much. When I hear that one of the major cities that I think of here in the United States is repealing housing restrictions that enable a specific real estate investment opportunity that previously wasn't allowed. That just immediately inspires an action to me. So therefore, we're going to communicate what's happening in Europe. I mean, again, who the fuck gives a shit what's happening in India? You know, it's like, we're not— that doesn't resonate with people. We talk about that third or fourth or like last, you know, it's like, technically we want to talk about it, but it's like. We don't really see a lot of people just show up to a call saying, 'Dude. India is doing co-living.' I can't believe it's here in America now. Like, I'm so excited. You know, it's like nobody fucking shows up and says that. But it helps to validate the claim of what we're attempting to communicate, that this has already been internationally validated. You understand? So we still talk about it, but we don't talk about it first. You know what I mean? So like, when you stack your flow here for your specific confirmation page, urgency-based video, you want to stack it with the most highly believable action-oriented reasons first. And then you kind of want to subsequently work down to the minority reasons after. And need I not say it? How would you think about that, Jeremy, from your end? If you're thinking about— I'd go to the compound interest calculator on investor. gov and I would show people if they only— Yeah. First of all, I'd open it up with this. I'd say— Who wants to retire when they're 65? And I would show some kind of data that's like a poll that pulled young people. That says. Nobody fucking wants to retire when they're 65 and everybody actually wants to retire in their 40s. And then I would say, let's look at what that would look like if you're currently in your 20s. And you actually wanted to retire in your 40s. And then I would go look at the compound interest calculator on investor. gov. I would then show some research. I'd say, look, the average person invests this much per month. So I would say, okay, let's start with that investment. Let's invest that amount per month. Let's say that you're 20 years old and you want to retire when you're 45. That gives you 25 years if you wanted to start this. And I would show what an annualized return with the S&P of about 10% would produce over 25 years with the average amount that somebody invests. I would then demonstrate to them that, if you only had, let's say, let's say you're 30 years old. And again, you wanted to retire in your 40s. And with those same numbers, what would the difference in the amount invested versus the amount returned be? I would then demonstrate that the amount of time that you have in the market to compound your money makes a substantial difference to your quality of life in the future and what age you're actually going to be able to retire. I would then demonstrate what's called the 4% rule. And I would showcase that you're technically only supposed to sell 4% of your portfolio balance at the age of retirement and every annual year following. And I would show how little money that truly is with less time in the market. I would also then showcase that you would therefore have to take much greater risks. If you had a shorter timeline in order to get a higher annualized return in order to come even remotely close, what time in the market compounding would demonstrate comparatively. That's a set of highly believable, mathematically based reasons that puts it into immediate perspective, something that's like upwards of 20 to 30 years away for some people, maybe 10 to 15 years for others. And the reality of their current situation. And that would move me into action to give a fuck about doing anything that relates to 20, 30 years from now, a lot more. Does that make sense? Okay. Yeah. Yeah. We do. That's an example. That's what inspired me when I was 24 years old to start at 25, putting up fucking 200 G's into the market every month. Cause I did that. Yeah, we hit that angle a lot. I guess the focus on the savings on the back end, because it's still going to be 10 to 15 to maybe 20 years, right? Like you mentioned. And so, like, sometimes people are like, man, you know, it's great, but like, it's so long away. They can't like— conceptualize that— so I'm what I'm saying: put it in, put it into reality, like demonstrate, dude, show them some fucking photos of old people that are greeters at Walmart, you know, that like to get like, just ask a simple thing. Literally start the video of the fucking cashier at Walmart who's like 80. Or like a waiter that's like 70 and say, 'Do you get sad when you see this?' You know what a lot of these people say? You know what a lot of these people say? When you ask these people, 'Why are you still working?' They say things like, you know, I'm just lonely. And I wanted to interact with people more. And that's a very common reason that these people give. But is it true? That's what you got to ask yourself. Is it true? How many people out there do you think gaslight each other about the real reasons for why they have to do what they do? Do you think that person actually? gives a fuck? Sure, they might like the interaction, but do you think they actually wanted to fucking be a Walmart greeter or a cashier or a fucking waiter? What do you feel in your chest? What kind of emotions go through your brain when you order DoorDash and the guy who comes to deliver your five guys? Is like fucking 70 in a beater car. What are you going to do one day when your parents come to you and they reveal that they never did retirement math and now you're responsible for their financial future until death? Because they were financially retarded. What are you going to do? You know, let me tell you what you need to do. And then you do everything that I just articulated to you in my prior explanation. Does that make sense? It's beautiful, yeah. Yep. Literally start the video just fucking a set of old people doing these jobs with like beater whips, you know, like fucked up houses, you know. It's like, well, what, how do you envision how do you envision your future when you when you get to the quote-unquote age of retiring, you know? Do you still want to fly in fucking seat 31C? In between two fat fucks on an economy flight. You know? Do you ever want to see your kids or do you want your kids to only come to you because you don't even have money to go to fly to them? You know, do you want to be able to go out to eat whenever the fuck you want when you're old? Or do you want to live on? Food stamps and ramen. You know, it's like, how do you picture your, how do you, do you actually think that far out? You know, do you, do you understand the reality of these situations? Where do you want to live? You want to live in, like, bumfuck Kansas? Cause that's all you can afford. You know, because you didn't take your financial future seriously. It's like, this is the reality of the situation for people who don't fucking think and act on retirement. In their years where they have the benefit of compounding money. Let me show you what compounding money looks like and the differences of it. And then bang. You get what I mean? Yep, spot on. Okay, very good. Timothy, you unmuted yourself. What'd you have? Yes, so it sounds like the key is basically like it can be something like the example that Brennan just gave, like that's something that affects them 20, 30 years in the future, but you have to drag it into the present in terms of how. For example, why it's urgent immediately right now. So, like, for the AI example, I just need to take that. And show them why what they do now impacts them. That's exactly right. Do any of you actually think to yourself, what I just said is true? Do any of the examples that I just provided in the example to Brennan actually resonate with you and would make you move into urgency? There's probably some of you fuckers right now that aren't investing into your financial retirement and you're retarded for doing that. And I hope that the reasons I just articulated inspire you to go watch the mastermind talks. Where I break down those mathematical reasons, my financial plan. And I hope that you actually do start taking it serious if you're not actually doing it. But I can't stress it enough to be clear. Those are all real reasons, okay? I get sick to my fucking stomach when I get some old fuck that comes to deliver my food. For the delivery of that day on Uber Eats or DoorDash, dog okay, I it hurts me. I told you guys this during COVID when we had the beach house up there in this little city called Ponce Inlet, for those of you who were a part of the group during that time. And if you weren't during COVID, four hours up the coast from Miami was this little beach house that I rented for about a year and a half. And it was full of boomers. I was all boomers. I was the youngest person by like 30 years. And there was a really sad reality of that situation. To be fair, this was— an inlet that had the beach on one side and a little river on the other. It had a handful of great restaurants. And the reality for those old people was pretty cool. They got to go to the beach and do fun things and drive around on golf carts. And you know what they legalized during COVID? They legalized drinks to go. Which meant that you could go to a restaurant. You know how much boomers love alcohol. And you could buy an alcoholic drink. And as long as it had a sticker on the lid, you were able to drive it home with you in your car. You know, so they essentially legalized pretty close to drinking and driving. The moral of the story is... I watched like these specific boomers have like a, you know, pretty good retirement, like the way they set themselves up. And in comparison, it's like, dude, I would drive to the other side of the bridge. On this like, you know, still coastal part of Florida. It was called Orange. And then the other part was Daytona Beach. And the average median income in Daytona Beach was like $41,000 a year. And I mean, dude. It looked like shit. You know, like these people lived in like the tiniest little shitbox houses. They had no money to fix anything. They had beater cars. I mean, dude, literally the reality for most of these old people was they'd sit in their home all day because they couldn't do anything else. It's not that they didn't want to do anything else. It's literally that they couldn't do anything else. They bank on urinized tax dollars to pay for their medical expenses. Bake on urinized tax dollars to pay for their social security benefits. They live off of like fucking a thousand to $3,000 a month, dude. Like they live devastatingly horrendous fucking lives for the ones that didn't plan for their financial futures. And. You know, the point I'm attempting to make with that is like, Dude, that pulls the reality of that situation right into the modern time for me when I'm sitting there looking at that kind of stuff. And that's an example of what we're talking about here, Timothy. I can take things that are so far into the future and I can pull them into right now. Like a lot of you guys, based on what you guys put as your urgency-based reasons in the chat. Related to AI. Like AI-based reasons. That AI is somehow going to fuck you. Or be a great opportunity for you. Well, it's like, how can I pull that into the right? You guys can't just say that fucking hundreds of thousands of people are going to lose their jobs. You know what you could say? You could literally show some examples of people who are quite literally right now losing their jobs, which there's plenty of. And that makes it a reality that people are actually experiencing now. It no longer makes it like a theory. It makes it something that's real. Okay. You have to make it real. OK, that's the difference between Jamil in this case saying. 'Hey, all these cities are gonna overcome their housing restrictions and enable co-living versus no dog, Austin, Texas, and like all these other fucking cities already did this shit.' You know, here's my portfolio. Here's how many I have. Here's how much money I already make. People are doing this now. You're like the train's already in motion, buddy. You can fucking run up to it and jump on still. But yeah, we're going to fucking pick up speed and you're not going to be able to jump on after a little bit longer. You know, you got to do this now. You see what I mean? Like you have to bring the things that are way off in the future into like right fucking now for the person to experience. You understand what I'm saying? Okay. Great. Does anybody else have questions on anything that we've spoken about here today? You can unmute yourself and ask away or you can drop it in the chat as well. Also, if you have any feedback that you'd like to share on some urgency-based reason that's worked well for you. Feel free to. Speak out loud about it now. Yeah, Jeremy, I have a quick question. Fire away, Michael. I wanted to know, for our service, one of the biggest problems we run into is, like, just multiple brands evaluating multiple agencies simultaneously. And so it's like we're kind of selling a similar solution. So would the urgency be to like. work with our team instead of urgency for the idea? Not necessarily. I mean, in that example, that's just more of like a unique mechanism where you just have to have some kind of differentiator between why somebody would pick you versus other people. The urgency-based motive would just inspire the action to work with somebody sooner than later, but it doesn't necessarily mean they'll bias towards you. The urgency, again, just creates prioritization to move somebody into action faster. Does that make sense? Is there, like, a good example of that? For our situation, go inside, go inside of the weekly group call, librarian, look at every, or ask the content finder bot about anything that relates to unique mechanisms. And, you know, consume that content in particular, because again, these two that you're asking about, they aren't correlated at all. There's also a great book called Breakthrough Advertising by Eugene Schwartz. most expensive book you'll buy. But one of the chapters specifically talks about market awareness and market sophistication, and breaks down exactly what we're talking about with unique mechanisms and late-stage market sophistication. You just have to articulate your process and your unique mechanism to stand out against others. People, as an example, work with me at a higher rate due to the fact that I have unique strategies, like my content ad strategy, backend selling systems. They also work with us, of course, because we have an extensive rap sheet of success and making people a fuckload of money, whether it's doing it hands-on or doing things in a setting like this. We're really good at getting people richer and we have a rap sheet of results to demonstrate that. The point I'm attempting to make is unique: the later stage market sophistication tactics of just revealing how we get people results. Does that make sense? The process. Yeah, that does. Thanks, Jeremy. Nice. Very good. Nick also just put into the chat. Manish doing a summary of breakthrough advertising. Nix Early stage YouTube content is all based on breakthrough advertising. So he has a lot of videos on that too. I'm sure he could be somebody that you could look to if you need content on it as it relates to e-comm examples. Thank you for sharing that, Nick. All right, very good. Does anybody else have anything? All right. Very good. It's a pleasure to talk with you all today. Congratulations on learning this. It's tremendously helpful and it should make a major impact on what you do. I want to reiterate: if you for some reason fuck this up, it's likely because you have reasons that nobody actually gives a fuck about that are not highly believable and therefore do not actually move people into action. Continue to rotate your reasons until you have the tremendous impact. Do not blame the strategy. Blame yourself if you fail. Highly effective. Talk soon.
